one_liner: A 58-year-old male smoker presents with progressive proximal leg weakness, difficulty rising from chairs, dry mouth, and diminished reflexes that transiently improve after brief exercise, with EMG showing incremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient has noticed progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a seated position over the past 3 months. He feels his legs are 'heavy' and weak, especially after prolonged sitting, but briefly improve when he starts walking (which he finds puzzling). He has also noticed dry mouth, constipation, erectile dysfunction (worsening over 6 months), and occasional blurred vision. He denies ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, or respiratory difficulty. He reports mild aching in his thighs. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and has not undergone any cancer screening. He lost 12 pounds unintentionally over the past 3 months. He was initially evaluated by his PCP who checked TSH and B12 (both normal) and referred him to neurology.
stage2.family_history: Father died of lung cancer at 62. Mother has type 2 diabetes.
stage2.social_history: Truck driver (currently on medical leave due to weakness). Active smoker, 40 pack-years. Drinks 2-3 beers on weekends. Divorced. No illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Thin man, appears older than stated age**; **Dry oral mucosa**. HEENT: **Pupils sluggishly reactive bilaterally**; **Extraocular movements full**; Cranial nerves intact. Neuro: **Motor — proximal weakness in lower extremities: hip flexion 4-/5, knee extension 4/5 bilaterally**; **Upper extremities: shoulder abduction 4+/5, grip 5/5**; **IMPORTANTLY: After 10 seconds of sustained maximum voluntary contraction of the quadriceps, strength briefly improves to 4+/5 (post-exercise facilitation)**; Sensory: Intact; DTRs: 1+ at biceps, absent (0) at knees and ankles; **IMPORTANTLY: After 10 seconds of vigorous quadriceps contraction, knee reflexes transiently return to 1+ (post-exercise facilitation of reflexes)**; **Gait: Mild waddling gait**; **Difficulty rising from chair without using arms (Gower-like maneuver)**; No ataxia; No fasciculations; No muscle wasting; **Lung exam: Decreased breath sounds at right apex**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild anemia; may be related to underlying malignancy (anemia of chronic disease)
Mild hyponatremia — could suggest SIADH from small cell lung cancer
DIAGNOSTIC: P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies are present in ~90% of LEMS patients and are the pathognomonic serological marker
SOX1 antibodies are highly associated with small cell lung cancer in LEMS patients (~95% specificity for paraneoplastic LEMS)
Negative; helps distinguish LEMS from myasthenia gravis
Elevated; nonspecific but consistent with underlying malignancy or inflammation

imaging_results.result: 2.8 cm right hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. Findings suspicious for primary lung malignancy.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Hilar mass in a heavy smoker with LEMS is highly concerning for small cell lung cancer (SCLC), which is the associated malignancy in ~50-60% of LEMS cases. SCLC is the most common paraneoplastic cause of LEMS.

imaging_results.result: FDG-avid right hilar mass (SUV max 12.4) with FDG-avid ipsilateral mediastinal lymph nodes. No distant metastases identified.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Staging suggests limited-stage SCLC. PET-CT is recommended for cancer screening in all LEMS patients.

key_tests: EMG with repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) — low-frequency (2-3 Hz) and high-frequency (50 Hz) or post-exercise
low_yield_tests: Tensilon (edrophonium) test (may have mild response but less useful than in MG)
stage3.low_yield_tests_note: The incremental (>100%) CMAP response after brief exercise or high-frequency RNS is the electrophysiological hallmark — it distinguishes LEMS from myasthenia gravis.

stage5.final_clue: EMG with repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) shows: Low baseline compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitudes. Decremental response at low-frequency (3 Hz) stimulation (15% decrement).

debrief.classic_presentation: Middle-aged male smoker with subacute progressive proximal leg weakness (legs > arms), areflexia with post-exercise facilitation (reflexes transiently return after brief exercise), and autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, constipation, erectile dysfunction, sluggish pupils). Weight loss and smoking history raise concern for paraneoplastic etiology (SCLC). EMG shows low CMAP amplitudes with incremental response (>100%) on high-frequency RNS or post-exercise.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This case tests the distinction between LEMS and myasthenia gravis (MG), which is the most critical differential. Key differences: (1) LEMS affects proximal LEGS predominantly (MG affects ocular/bulbar muscles first), (2) LEMS has areflexia with post-exercise FACILITATION (MG has normal or brisk reflexes), (3) LEMS has autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, constipation) — MG does not, (4) LEMS shows INCREMENTAL response on RNS (MG shows decremental), (5) LEMS has VGCC antibodies (MG has AChR or MuSK antibodies). The attending-level pitfall is failing to screen for SCLC — ~50-60% of LEMS is paraneoplastic, and SCLC may be occult at presentation. Guidelines recommend CT chest at diagnosis and repeat screening every 6 months for 2 years. Also critical: avoid aminoglycosides, magnesium, and calcium channel blockers in LEMS — they can precipitate crisis by further impairing presynaptic calcium-dependent neurotransmitter release.
debrief.pathophysiology: LEMS is caused by IgG autoantibodies against P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs) on the presynaptic motor nerve terminal. In normal neuromuscular transmission, an action potential triggers opening of presynaptic VGCCs, calcium influx triggers acetylcholine vesicle fusion and release. In LEMS, anti-VGCC antibodies reduce the number of functional calcium channels via cross-linking, internalization, and complement-mediated destruction. This decreases calcium influx and ACh release (presynaptic defect). With repeated stimulation or exercise, cumulative calcium builds up in the terminal, temporarily increasing ACh release — explaining post-exercise facilitation. Autonomic symptoms arise from VGCC antibodies targeting autonomic nerve terminals (parasympathetic > sympathetic). In paraneoplastic LEMS, VGCCs on SCLC tumor cells are the inciting antigen — the immune response against tumor VGCCs cross-reacts with nerve terminal VGCCs.
debrief.treatment_overview: 1. Treat underlying malignancy (if paraneoplastic): Chemotherapy/radiation for SCLC often improves LEMS symptoms as tumor burden decreases. 2. Symptomatic: 3,4-Diaminopyridine (amifampridine, FDA-approved for LEMS) blocks presynaptic potassium channels, prolonging the action potential and increasing calcium influx and ACh release. Dose: 15-80 mg/day in divided doses. 3. Immunotherapy (for refractory cases or autoimmune LEMS): IVIg, plasma exchange, prednisone, azathioprine, rituximab. 4. Pyridostigmine (cholinesterase inhibitor) provides modest additional benefit when combined with amifampridine. 5. Cancer screening: CT chest at diagnosis, repeat every 6 months for 2 years (if initially negative). 6. Avoid drugs that worsen NMJ transmission: aminoglycosides, magnesium, calcium channel blockers, botulinum toxin.
high_yield_pearls: LEMS is a PRESYNAPTIC disorder (antibodies against VGCC on the nerve terminal) while MG is a POSTSYNAPTIC disorder (antibodies against AChR on the muscle). This explains why LEMS shows incremental response and MG shows decremental response on RNS.

Post-exercise facilitation is the clinical hallmark of LEMS: reflexes that are initially absent become transiently elicitable after brief exercise, and strength briefly improves with use — the OPPOSITE of MG where fatigable weakness worsens with use.

Up to 50-60% of LEMS cases are paraneoplastic (SCLC) — ALL patients diagnosed with LEMS must undergo CT chest and cancer surveillance every 6 months for 2 years, even if initial screening is negative.
SOX1 antibody positivity in a LEMS patient has ~95% specificity for underlying SCLC — it helps identify paraneoplastic cases early.

Amifampridine (3,4-DAP) is the FDA-approved first-line symptomatic treatment for LEMS — it works by blocking presynaptic potassium channels, prolonging nerve terminal depolarization, and increasing calcium entry and ACh release.
sources: Titulaer MJ, et al. Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome: from clinical characteristics to therapeutic strategies. Lancet Neurol. 2011
AAEM Practice Parameter: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome, 2020
UpToDate: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome — clinical features and diagnosis
